subject: english
learning_objectives: Define a sentence and identify its essential components
Distinguish between simple, compound, multiple, complex, and compound-complex sentences
Construct each sentence type with correct examples

sections.heading: Definition
sections.content: A sentence is a group of words that contains a subject and a verb and expresses a complete sense or thought.

sections.heading: Simple Sentence
sections.content:
Contains one subject and one predicate (verb). It is made up of one main clause. Simple sentences may be long but have only one subject-verb unit.

Examples:
  The woman ran.
  He is in the room.
  The thief has been caught.

sections.heading: Compound Sentence
sections.content:
Contains two main clauses linked by a co-ordinating conjunction (and, but, or).

Examples:
  The work stops [but] the tools are kept handy.
  Ngozi found the goat [and] she took it to her mother.

sections.heading: Multiple Sentence
sections.content:
Contains more than two main clauses linked by co-ordinating conjunctions.

Example:
  The child climbed onto my laps, urinated on my trousers, [and] smiled at me happily.
  The choir master returned from church [and] asked for his flute [but] it could not be found.

sections.heading: Complex Sentence
sections.content:
Contains one main clause and one or more subordinate clauses.

Examples:
  When it rains, [subordinate] he does not go to work. [main]
  Grace did not do the assignment [main] which the English teacher gave her. [subordinate]

sections.heading: Compound-Complex Sentence
sections.content:
Contains two or more main clauses and at least one subordinate clause.

Example:
  The work stops [main] when it rains [subordinate] but the tools are kept handy. [main]
